terms.definition: Combining two branches by creating a new commit whose parents are the tips of both branches; preserves divergent history.
Merge strategy that uses the common ancestor of the two branches as a base to decide which changes came from which side; the default Git merge algorithm.
Degenerate merge where the target branch hasn't moved since the divergence; Git just slides the branch pointer forward without making a merge commit.
A commit with two (or more) parent pointers, marking the convergence of two branches.
Operation that replays a series of commits on top of a different base commit, producing new commits with new SHAs.
`git rebase -i` — opens an editor allowing you to reorder, squash, fixup, edit, drop or reword commits before they're replayed.
Combine multiple commits into one (during interactive rebase or 'squash and merge' on a PR).
Apply the changes from a specific commit onto the current branch as a new commit.
Overwrite a remote branch with local history; required after a rebase. `--force-with-lease` adds a safety check.
